aliases: Warm Chocolate Cake with Molten Center

english: Warm Chocolate Cake with Molten Center

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for chocolate lava cake; closest match is chocolate cake food product (FOODON_03301710). The molten-center characteristic is not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cakes and pastries broadly; no specific code exists for chocolate lava cake or molten chocolate cake.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chocolate lava cake as a food concept.

preparation_method: partially baked to retain molten center

serving.natural_language_portion: One individual chocolate lava cake typically weighs approximately 90 g and is served warm as a single-portion dessert, often accompanied by vanilla ice cream or whipped cream.

nutrient_source.title: Chocolate lava cake — Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for chocolate lava cake (cr_id F02010008). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 12.0 g/100g based on typical butter and dark chocolate composition consistent with HPB data; monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and other micronutrients not reported in HPB source and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Chocolate cake, made from packet mix — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to chocolate lava cake
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chocolate lava cake in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 40 is estimated by analogy to chocolate cake entries (GI range 38–45) in the University of Sydney GI Database, which reflect the moderating effect of fat and protein on glucose absorption. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 27.45) / 100 ≈ 11.0, using 90 g serving size (27.45 g carbohydrate per serving). Input record GI value of 40 and GL of 12.2 are consistent with this analogous estimate; GL recalculated here using 90 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sugar content (23.8 g/100g) and high saturated fat (~12 g/100g); sodium is relatively low at 53 mg/100g but does not offset the cardiovascular risk from sugar and saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chocolate lava cake due to its very high sugar content (23.8 g/100g), which independently raises blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines, and its high saturated fat load (~12 g/100g from butter and dark chocolate), which contributes to arterial stiffness; occasional consumption of a very small portion (≤45 g) may be tolerated but is not recommended.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 23.8 g/100g exceeds the 20 g/100g hard gate for type 2 diabetes; despite 2.5 g dietary fibre and a low estimated GI of 40, the absolute sugar load per serving is clinically significant
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chocolate lava cake is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes; the sugar content of 23.8 g/100g (approximately 21.4 g per 90 g serving) will cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and even a small portion delivers a substantial glycaemic load; this food should be avoided or reserved for very rare occasions with careful carbohydrate budgeting under dietitian supervision.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12 g/100g from butter and dark chocolate) and very high sugar (23.8 g/100g) both contribute to LDL elevation, endothelial dysfunction, and increased cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid chocolate lava cake due to its high saturated fat content (~12 g/100g), which raises LDL cholesterol, and its very high sugar content (23.8 g/100g), which promotes triglyceride synthesis and endothelial inflammation; this is a calorie-dense dessert (336 kcal/100g) that is inconsistent with a heart-healthy dietary pattern and should be consumed only rarely and in very small portions if at all.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (~12 g/100g from butter) and very high refined sugar (23.8 g/100g) are both pro-inflammatory and trigger RA dietary hard gates
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chocolate lava cake is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the high saturated fat from butter (~12 g/100g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and the very high refined sugar content (23.8 g/100g) further amplifies inflammatory mediators; this food should be avoided, particularly during RA flares.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chocolate lava cake, also known as molten chocolate cake or fondant au chocolat, is a warm individual-portion dessert consisting of a partially baked chocolate cake with a liquid, flowing molten chocolate center that is revealed when the cake is cut open. It is made from dark chocolate, butter, eggs, sugar, and a small amount of plain flour, baked at high temperature for a short time so that the exterior sets while the interior remains liquid. The texture contrasts a firm, slightly crisp outer shell with a rich, intensely chocolatey molten core. Per 100 g, it provides 336 kcal, 6 g protein, 21.13 g fat (approximately 12 g saturated fat from butter and dark chocolate), 30.5 g carbohydrate, and 23.8 g sugar, with 2.5 g dietary fibre and 53 mg sodium. The high sugar and saturated fat content make it unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. Despite a low estimated glycemic index of approximately 40 (moderated by fat and protein content), the absolute sugar load per serving is clinically significant. The dish originated in French cuisine and is widely served in restaurants and cafes globally, including Singapore, as a premium dessert. It is typically served warm with vanilla ice cream or whipped cream. The dark chocolate component contributes iron (3.25 mg/100g) and calcium (42 mg/100g) but these benefits are outweighed by the high sugar and saturated fat content in a clinical dietary context.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F02010008) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at ~12 g/100g based on typical dark chocolate and butter composition, consistent with the HPB data profile. GI estimated by analogy to chocolate cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. All values are internally consistent and plausible for this food type.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for chocolate lava cake are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F02010008) as of 2026-04-16. Saturated fat of 12.0 g/100g is an estimate derived from typical butter and dark chocolate composition and is not directly reported in the HPB source; this value should be verified against a laboratory analysis or USDA FoodData Central entry for a comparable product. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on chocolate cake entries in the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chocolate lava cake was found in the database as of 2026-04-16. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as 11.0 using 90 g serving size; the input record GL of 12.2 used a slightly different serving assumption. This food is classified as Western / French-origin and does not require Singaporean multilingual naming (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) under the Project Veritas v2 multilingual rules. No mandatory local-language names apply for this cuisine category.
